section: A High-Flux Fast Molten Salt Reactor for the Transmutation of Caesium-137 and Strontium-90
text: A High-Flux Fast Molten Salt Reactor for the Transmutation of Caesium-137 and Strontium-90

section: A High-Flux Fast Molten Salt Reactor for the Transmutation of Caesium-137 and Strontium-90
text: A High-Flux Fast Molten Salt Reactor for the Transmutation of Caesium-137 and Strontium-90
A high flux molten salt (plutonium chlorides) fast reactor (7 GWth) with internal thermal zone for transmutation of Sr-90 and Cs-137 is here discussed.
These fission products have been produced by breeder reactors with total power of 23 GWth and by the 7 GWth fast burner reactor.
For the case when the power breeder reactors achieve a breeding gain $G > 0.2$ the doubling time for whole system, including the high-flux burner reactor, equals ~30 years.

The transmutation of Sr-90 and Cs-137 in a total flux of $3,8 \cdot 10^{16}$ n cm $^{-2}$ s $^{-1}$ , and thermal flux $\sim 2,0 \cdot 10^{16}$ n cm $^{-2}$ s $^{-1}$ can achieve the steady-state which corresponds to an effective half-life of 1,8 year for Sr-90 and of 8,9 years for Cs-137.
In terms of hazard coefficient the transmutation system gives an improvement of 14 times.
The aim of this work was to study the concept of transmitting Sr-90 and Cs-137 in a very high-flux reactor.
The half-lives (28 years for Sr-90 and 30 years for Cs-137) indicate the longevity of the isotopes.
To "significantly" reduce their activity (by a factor of 1000) through natural decay would take 300 years.
These facts have understandably prompted numerous transmutation studies (Schneider 1974; see also Taube, 1975).
The most optimistic has been transmutation in a controlled thermonuclear reactor CTR (Wolkenhauer, 1973).
However, even unrealistic flux levels in CTR could only reduce the transmutation half-life from 30 years down to 5-15 years.

Furthermore, Lidsky (1975) has pointed out that for any reasonable ratio of fusion burners to fission reactors, the burners soon contain a much higher radioactive burden than the fission reactors themselves.
These requirements lead towards the inclusion in the system of a molten salt burner reactor with a central flux-trap (Taube 1974).
Such a system of $\mathrm{L} + 1$ reactors should provide a doubling time $T \leq 30$ years; this is estimated to satisfy the needs of future world power development.

As is well known, light materials can scatter neutrons past the neutron-absorbing intermediate-energy resonance region. ${}_{1}^{H}$ is the most efficient nuclide in this respect but also exhibits appreciable thermal absorption. $^{2}D$ , $^{9}Be$ and $^{12}C$ are usual alternatives. $^{8}0$ is a bit heavy though frequently already present in a molecular combination.
Figure 4 shows that just a small proportion of H molar fraction has a large deleterious effect on the Cs-137 transmutation rate.
As Sr-90 and Cs-137 also have their fair share of resonances it is advantageous to thermalize the flux before reaching the flux trap region containing these targets.
Be (and D) compounds, of course, have also to their advantage a relatively low (n,2n) threshold (1.67 MeV).
Replacement of Be by C or Mo wall material should therefore lower the FP transmutation rate, and it does (Table 3).
Figure 5 indicates an optimum thickness of about 5 cm Be.

Reactor calculations were made for 5 to 100 micron-thick intermediate walls as well as for distributed boron inside the fast region.
As seen from Figure 7 the FP transmutation rate is always reduced; although the spectrum in the fuel region is harder, the loss of thermal neutrons to the flux trap has a greater effect.
The disadvantages arise from the increased fuel inventory in the burner, e.g. a factor $B_{I} = 2 \pm 0.5$ higher.
Figure 8 shows the effect of $\mathrm{PuCl}_3$ molar fraction upon specific power, core exit temperature, and Cs-137 transmutation rate.

section: 7. Remarks about transmutation and hazard coefficients
text: 17) i.e. in the lifetime of one reactor generation (for more exact considerations see (Taube, Ligou, Bucher, 1975).
These considerations show that a high flux burner reactor for the transmutation of selected most hazardous fission products as Sr-90 and Cs-137 may be achieved for reasonable assumptions:
1) the whole system is a steady state transmutation system
2) in the steady-state the amount of transmutated nuclides is more than one order of magnitude lower than in a spontaneously beta-decay steady-state.
4) the whole system is still breeding, with a relatively long doubling time of 30 years.

The relative high specific power-rating of $1\mathrm{kgPu/MWth}$ , inclusive of the amount of plutonium out of core (cooling transport, reprocessing) is rather optimistic but not impossible, especially if a more sophisticated system will be used e.g.
